description: The supplied image is a publisher 'Check for updates' badge bearing a bookmark icon; it contains no scientific micrographs, diffraction patterns, spectra, or data plots, so no visual materials-science information can be directly extracted. Relying solely on the accompanying text extraction summary, the study examines Fe-8Mn-3Al-0.2C (M1) and Fe-8Mn-3Al-1Si-0.2C (M2) steels after homogenization, hot rolling, and intercritical annealing at 700°C for 1 h. After intercritical annealing, M1 exhibits 45% retained austenite, UTS of 867 MPa, and total elongation of 71.8% (PSE ~61 GPa%), whereas M2 shows 19% retained austenite, UTS of 698 MPa, and 59.4% elongation (PSE ~41 GPa%). Post-fracture EBSD indicates austenite fractions drop to 6.5% for M1 and 3.2% for M2, confirming deformation-induced martensitic transformation. Hot-rolled counterparts display much higher strength but brittle cleavage fracture. These reported trends cannot be verified from the provided image pixels.

This figure presents six thermodynamic equilibrium phase-fraction plots calculated with ThermoCalc (TCFE9) for the Fe–8Mn–0.2C base alloy with systematic Al and Si additions: a) 0Al, b) 1Al, c) 2Al, d) 3Al, e) 4Al, and f) 3Al–1Si. Each subplot shows predicted phase volume fractions versus temperature (25–1400 °C) for BCC, FCC, CEMENTITE, M23C6, and LIQUID. The yellow-highlighted intercritical temperature (ICT) range is anchored at 600 °C and visibly broadens with increasing Al: 600–688 °C for 0Al, 600–730 °C for 1Al, 600–789 °C for 2Al, 600–900 °C for 3Al, 600–980 °C for 4Al, and 600–1000 °C for 3Al–1Si. For the 0–3Al compositions, BCC ferrite continuously transforms to FCC austenite across the ICT window, while cementite and M23C6 fully dissolve at or below 600 °C. For 4Al and 3Al–1Si, the text states that δ-ferrite is introduced as an equilibrium phase in the ICT range; the corresponding curves show the FCC fraction peaking within the ICT window and subsequently declining as BCC (annotated δ) re-stabilizes at elevated temperatures before melting occurs. These calculations establish that Al addition widens the austenite-plus-ferrite two-phase region and that higher Al or Al+Si promotes δ-ferrite formation, thereby rationalizing the selection of 3Al (without δ) and 3Al–1Si (with δ) for subsequent experimental investigation.

This figure presents thermodynamic equilibrium predictions for two steels labeled M1 and M2, showing how intercritical annealing (IA) temperature affects the equilibrium austenite fraction and austenite composition. Panel (a) plots the predicted equilibrium austenite fraction (γeq) versus temperature from ~625 °C to 900 °C, with the black dashed M1 curve lying above the red dashed M2 curve throughout the range. At the selected intercritical annealing temperature (IAT) of 700 °C, marked by a vertical dashed line, M1 reaches roughly 48 % austenite and M2 approximately 31 %. Panel (b) shows the corresponding predicted weight percentages of Mn, Al, Si, and C within austenite as functions of temperature. For both steels, Mn content decreases with increasing temperature to roughly 11 % at 700 °C, whereas Al increases moderately; C remains low and relatively stable, and Si appears only in M2. The caption and text together establish that this figure was used to select 700 °C as the IAT, balancing the desire for a high equilibrium austenite fraction against the need to retain sufficient Mn and C in austenite to prevent its decomposition to martensite during cooling. Consequently, 700 °C represents the thermodynamic compromise adopted for subsequent processing.

This figure presents a schematic illustration of the complete thermo-mechanical processing route for two low-density medium-Mn steels, M1 (Fe-8Mn-3Al-0.2C) and M2 (Fe-8Mn-3Al-1Si-0.2C), accompanied by SEM micrographs captured after each processing stage. The temperature-time profile shows: as-cast samples subjected to homogenization at 1100°C followed by water quenching, subsequent hot rolling at 1100°C with air cooling, and final intercritical annealing at 700°C for 1 h followed by water quenching. Corresponding micrographs display the microstructural evolution: M1-AC/M2-AC (as-cast), M1-HQ/M2-HQ (homogenized and quenched), M1-HR/M2-HR (hot-rolled and air-cooled), and M1-IA/M2-IA (intercritically annealed), with scale bars ranging from 3 μm to 500 μm. The images reveal distinct phase morphologies at each step, with M2 exhibiting δ-ferrite formation due to Si addition, while M1 develops a finer dual-phase ferrite-austenite structure after intercritical annealing. This figure establishes the processing-microstructure relationship, demonstrating that Si-induced δ-ferrite significantly alters austenite fraction and stability during intercritical annealing, thereby governing the final mechanical performance.

Figure 4 presents combined secondary-electron SEM micrographs and X-ray diffraction patterns for hot-rolled Fe–8Mn–3Al–0.2C (M1-HR) and Fe–8Mn–3Al–1Si–0.2C (M2-HR) medium-Mn steels. Panels (a1) and (a2) display the M1-HR microstructure consisting of polygonal α-ferrite and lath-type martensitic-austenitic (α′+γ) phases, with (a2) providing a higher-magnification view of the lath morphology. Panels (b1)–(b3) reveal the M2-HR microstructure containing δ-ferrite, α-ferrite, and a very fine martensitic-austenitic (α′+γ) phase, where (b2) and (b3) show successively higher magnifications highlighting the fine secondary phases. Panel (c) compares XRD profiles of both samples, confirming the presence of ferrite/martensite and austenite; quantitative analysis indicates austenite fractions of 5.1 % for M1-HR and 5.7 % for M2-HR. The figure collectively demonstrates that Si addition promotes δ-ferrite formation and refines the martensitic-austenitic constituent in the hot-rolled condition, while both steels retain only limited austenite after air cooling from hot rolling.

Figure 5 presents the microstructural characterization of the intercritically annealed Fe-8Mn-3Al-0.2C steel (M1-IA). Panel (a) comprises four secondary-electron SEM micrographs (a1–a4) at progressively higher magnifications with scale bars of 20 μm, 5 μm, 3 μm, and 2 μm, respectively, revealing a dual-phase microstructure consisting of polygonal α-ferrite (marked α, appearing as relatively smooth regions) and a fine lamellar α′+γ constituent (marked α′+γ). The images indicate negligible martensite, consistent with intercritical annealing at 700 °C for 1 h followed by water quenching. Panel (b) is an EBSD phase map of the same M1-IA sample, where the orange-colored phase represents austenite distributed within the ferrite matrix. Together, these images confirm that the M1-IA sample contains only α-ferrite and austenite without δ-ferrite. Citation text reports quantitative phase analysis yields 45 % retained austenite in M1-IA, substantially higher than the 19 % reported for the Si-bearing M2-IA counterpart, supporting the conclusion that the absence of Si prevents equilibrium δ-ferrite formation and maximizes austenite fraction and stability.

Figure 6 presents a multimodal microstructural characterization of the intercritically annealed Fe-8Mn-3Al-1Si-0.2C (M2-IA) sample. Panel (a) contains four SEM micrographs at progressively increasing magnifications: (a1) at a 200 μm scale shows a low-magnification overview with delta-ferrite (δ) indicated by an arrow and regions labeled α′+γ+α; (a2) at a 20 μm scale resolves α′+γ colonies alongside α-ferrite; (a3) at a 5 μm scale reveals fine lamellar α′+γ structures adjacent to α; and the fourth image (bearing a repeated a3 label in the figure but corresponding to a4 per the caption) at a 3 μm scale provides the highest magnification view of the α′+γ and α morphology. Panel (b) is an EBSD phase map at a 5 μm scale where the orange-colored phase represents austenite, illustrating its spatial distribution within the ferritic matrix. Together, these images demonstrate that the M2-IA microstructure comprises α-ferrite, retained austenite, and coarse band-type δ-ferrite, with the SEM images confirming the presence of fine martensite-austenite (α′+γ) regions and the EBSD map directly visualizing austenite retention. This supports the conclusion that 1 wt.% Si addition stabilizes δ-ferrite during intercritical annealing at 700 °C, reducing the retained austenite fraction compared to the Si-free M1 counterpart and establishing the phase constitution responsible for the observed mechanical behavior.

Figure 7 presents overlaid X-ray diffraction patterns of two intercritically annealed low-density medium Mn steels: M1-IA (Fe-8Mn-3Al-0.2C, black curve) and M2-IA (Fe-8Mn-3Al-1Si-0.2C, red curve), both processed at 700°C for 1 h and water quenched. The patterns span 2θ from 40° to 110° and display peaks corresponding to body-centered-cubic α-ferrite (α 110, α 200, α 211, α 220) and face-centered-cubic austenite (γ 111, γ 200, γ 220, γ 311, γ 222). The M1-IA sample exhibits substantially stronger austenite reflections and a quantitative phase fraction of fγ = 45.6%, while the M2-IA sample shows a dominant α 110 peak and a much lower retained austenite fraction of fγ = 19.6%. This direct comparison confirms that 1 wt.% Si addition drastically reduces austenite retention after intercritical annealing, which the caption and text attribute to the stabilization of coarse band-type δ-ferrite in M2-IA and altered Mn partitioning, thereby governing austenite stability and the resulting mechanical properties.

Figure 8 presents a multi-panel analysis of austenite stability and microstructural heterogeneity for intercritically annealed low-density medium-Mn steels M1 (Fe-8Mn-3Al-0.2C) and M2 (Fe-8Mn-3Al-1Si-0.2C). Panels (a1) and (a2) display the calculated retained austenite fraction at room temperature (left y-axis, solid squares) and the corresponding Ms temperatures (right y-axis, open squares) as functions of intercritical annealing temperature (600–900 °C), obtained using the Koistinen-Marburger model. A critical temperature near 650 °C is evident: in the shaded lower-temperature regime the Ms remains below room temperature, permitting complete retention of the equilibrium austenite fraction, whereas above this threshold the rising Ms promotes partial austenite-to-martensite transformation upon cooling. Panel (b) compares the grain size distribution of M1 and M2, showing that M1-IA contains a higher frequency of fine grains below 0.5 μm, which enhances local austenite stability. Panel (c) provides a schematic of elemental partitioning during intercritical annealing, where Mn and C partition from α-ferrite into austenite (γ) in both steels (red arrows), while M2 additionally exhibits Si partitioning from austenite into δ-ferrite and α-ferrite (black arrows), with arrow density indicating partitioning extent. Collectively, the figure rationalizes why the experimentally observed retained austenite fractions after IA at 700 °C (~45 % for M1 and ~19 % for M2) substantially exceed the K-M calculated values (~16 % and ~11 %), attributing the discrepancy to the combined effects of sub-micron grain refinement and local solute segregation.

This figure presents a three-panel schematic illustrating the temporal evolution of manganese concentration profiles across the moving α/γ interface during intercritical annealing at 700 °C in low-density medium Mn steels (Fe-8Mn-3Al-0.2C and Fe-8Mn-3Al-1Si-0.2C). Panel (a) depicts the initial condition (t=0) when the sample is just brought to the intercritical annealing temperature (IAT), showing uniform Mn concentrations in the austenite (γ, shaded region) and ferrite (α/δ) phases with a sharp step at the interface. Panel (b) shows the intermediate state at t=t1, where local equilibrium is established at the interface, yielding equilibrium concentrations C_Mn^γ(eq) and C_Mn^α/δ(eq), while diffusion-limited concentration gradients develop in both phases. Panel (c) represents the ideal final equilibrium condition (t=∞) with flat concentration profiles in each phase. The caption and text note that because the actual annealing time is limited to 1 h, the system does not fully reach equilibrium, so local Mn enrichment persists in austenite and concentration gradients remain, which critically influences retained austenite stability and fraction.

This figure combines engineering stress–strain curves and SEM fractography for low-density medium Mn steels M1 (Fe-8Mn-3Al-0.2C) and M2 (Fe-8Mn-3Al-1Si-0.2C) after hot rolling (HR) and intercritical annealing (IA). Panels (a) and (d) plot stress versus strain: visually, HR samples show high ultimate tensile strength with limited elongation, while IA samples display lower strength but markedly higher ductility. Panels (b) and (e) are SEM images of HR fractures, revealing flat facets in M1-HR and river markings in M2-HR, characteristic of brittle cleavage. Panels (c) and (f) show IA fracture surfaces dominated by dimple rupture, indicating ductile failure. As reported in the caption, M1-HR achieves a UTS of 1712±10 MPa and total elongation of 14.8±0.5%, whereas M1-IA reaches 867±10 MPa and 71.8±0.5%; M2-HR shows 1055±15 MPa and 15.4±0.8%, while M2-IA shows 698±20 MPa and 59.4±0.6%. The figure supports the conclusion that HR samples are brittle due to martensite, whereas IA samples exhibit an excellent strength-ductility balance via the TRIP effect from retained austenite, with reported PSE values of 61 GPa% for M1-IA and 41 GPa% for M2-IA.

Figure 11 correlates the mechanical response and microstructural evolution during tensile deformation of intercritically annealed low-density medium-Mn steels: M1-IA (Fe-8Mn-3Al-0.2C) and M2-IA (Fe-8Mn-3Al-1Si-0.2C). Panels (a) and (b) present true stress / strain-hardening rate (dσ/dε) versus true strain curves and retained austenite fraction decay, respectively. M1-IA shows a higher strain-hardening rate (1338 MPa at point A) and slower austenite decomposition (fγ = 45.6·e^−3.6ε) compared with M2-IA (989 MPa at point B; fγ = 19.6·e^−3.8ε). Panels (c) and (d) are EBSD phase maps after fracture, indicating residual austenite fractions of 6.5 vol.% (M1-IA) and 3.2 vol.% (M2-IA), with green denoting austenite and red ferrite, confirming substantial deformation-induced martensitic transformation. Panels (e–h) are kernel average misorientation (KAM) maps (scale Min 0 to Max 5) before and after tensile testing. Both alloys exhibit increased KAM values post-deformation, signifying extensive TRIP effect; however, M2-IA (h) displays marked strain partitioning between low-KAM δ-ferrite regions and high-KAM transformed martensite/austenite areas. Overall, the figure demonstrates that the absence of Si in M1-IA preserves higher austenite stability and a sustained gradual TRIP effect, whereas Si-bearing M2-IA suffers from reduced austenite retention and heterogeneous strain distribution due to equilibrium δ-ferrite formation.

This schematic illustrates the TRIP (Transformation-Induced Plasticity) effect as the dominant deformation mechanism in low-density medium Mn steels under increasing applied strain (ε). The diagram presents a three-stage temporal evolution: (left) an early deformation stage showing austenite grains labeled γc (undergoing TRIP to become α′ martensite) and γf (undergoing dislocation glide followed by TRIP), adjacent to an α-ferrite grain; the caption states that red arrows indicate Mn partitioning from ferrite into austenite; (middle) at intermediate strain, γc has fully transformed to lath-type α′ martensite while γf accumulates dislocations (glide activity); (right) at higher strain, γf subsequently transforms to α′ martensite via TRIP. The figure distinguishes two austenite populations with distinct transformation kinetics, supporting the core conclusion that retained austenite progressively transforms to martensite during tensile deformation in the investigated Fe-8Mn-3Al-(1Si)-0.2C steels, thereby providing sustained strain hardening and enhanced ductility after intercritical annealing.
